concepts.name: Propositional Logic
concepts.description: Modeling claims and the connectives between them — implication, equivalence, negation — without looking inside the claims.

concepts.name: Linked Premises
concepts.description: Premises that only work together: neither implies the conclusion alone (modeled as an #Implies:key bundle).

concepts.name: Case Analysis
concepts.description: Proving C from (A or B) by showing each disjunct implies C separately — disjunction needs no special edge, just two #Implies into the same conclusion.

sources.title: forall x: Calgary — An Introduction to Formal Logic
sources.author: Magnus, Button, et al.

snippets.note: Premise 1 of the conjoint pair — alone it implies nothing about whether death is bad.

links.to: Death, the most awful of evils, is nothing to us.

snippets.text: All good and evil consists in sensation.
snippets.note: Premise 2 — only TOGETHER with premise 1 does the conclusion follow, hence the shared #Implies:epicurus bundle key.

links.to: Death, the most awful of evils, is nothing to us.

snippets.text: So long as we exist, death is not with us.
snippets.note: First horn of the dilemma: you are either alive or dead, and each case alone implies the conclusion — so two BARE #Implies edges, no bundle.

links.to: Death, the most awful of evils, is nothing to us.

snippets.text: When death comes, then we do not exist.
snippets.note: Second horn — (alive or dead) implies the conclusion, decomposed as case analysis.

links.to: Death, the most awful of evils, is nothing to us.

snippets.text: Death, the most awful of evils, is nothing to us.
snippets.note: The conclusion both structures land on: a conjoint bundle AND a case analysis, in one small argument.
raises: Can something be bad for you without ever being experienced by you?

snippets.text: Death is an evil because it deprives us of the goods that continued life would have brought.
snippets.note: The deprivation account: a direct denial of Epicurus's conclusion — #Negates, not merely #Opposes, because at most one of the two claims can hold.

links.to: Death, the most awful of evils, is nothing to us.

answers: Can something be bad for you without ever being experienced by you?

syllabi.title: Modeling Arguments: Propositional Logic
syllabi.goal: Learn the propositional-logic framework by diagramming one classic argument: conjoint premises (#Implies:key), case analysis (two bare #Implies), and negation (#Negates) — all on Epicurus vs Nagel.
order: forall x: Calgary — An Introduction to Formal Logic
